attributes.stem:
A 24-year-old man presents to the General Practice clinic for review of recently diagnosed Crohn's disease. He initially presented three months ago with intermittent abdominal pain and bloody diarrhoea. He has no other significant medical history and takes no regular medications. He is a non-smoker and drinks two standard drinks of alcohol per day.

Colonoscopy two months ago revealed extensive shallow ulceration in the colon and distal ileum. Magnetic resonance imaging showed one additional segment of ileal involvement without evidence of perianal disease.

He achieved clinical remission with a three-month course of prednisone and now requires maintenance therapy.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate maintenance therapy?
attributes.explanation:
This patient has moderate to severe Crohn's disease based on the extent of disease (involvement of both colon and ileum) and endoscopic findings of extensive ulceration. While he has achieved remission with corticosteroids, his disease characteristics suggest a higher risk of relapse without appropriate maintenance therapy.

Azathioprine is the most appropriate first-line maintenance therapy in this setting. It has been shown to maintain remission in about 70% of patients and reduces the need for corticosteroids. The patient's young age and extent of disease make him an ideal candidate for thiopurine therapy.

Mesalazine has not shown consistent benefit in maintaining remission in Crohn's disease, particularly in patients with moderate to severe disease. This is in contrast to its use in ulcerative colitis, where it is a mainstay of therapy.

Prednisone is effective for inducing remission but is not appropriate for maintenance therapy due to significant long-term side effects including osteoporosis, diabetes, and increased infection risk. This is particularly important given the patient's young age and likely need for long-term therapy.

Infliximab, while effective, is typically reserved as second-line therapy when thiopurines alone are insufficient. However, early introduction may be considered in high-risk patients (such as those with perianal disease, stricturing or penetrating complications) which are not present in this case.

Ustekinumab is generally used as third-line therapy after failure of both thiopurines and TNF inhibitors. Its use as first-line maintenance therapy would be difficult to justify from both a clinical and cost perspective.

Regular monitoring of blood counts and liver function is required for patients on azathioprine. The therapeutic response should be assessed at 3-6 months, with consideration of step-up therapy if remission is not maintained.
learningPoints: Azathioprine is first-line maintenance therapy in Crohn's disease after induction of remission with corticosteroids.
Corticosteroids are effective for inducing remission but are not appropriate for maintenance therapy.
Anti-TNF therapy may be considered early in high-risk patients to prevent complications.
Disease extent and severity guide maintenance therapy choices in Crohn's disease.
references: Therapeutic Guidelines. Melbourne: Therapeutic Guidelines Limited. Available from: https://www.tg.org.au
Shi YH et al. The state of the art on treatment of Crohn’s disease. J Gastroenterol. 2018; 53(9): 989–998
Lichtenstein GR, Loftus EV, Isaacs KL, et al. ACG Clinical Guideline: Management of Crohn’s Disease in Adults. Am J Gastroenterol 2018; 113:481–517.
